sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Stephanie Morales, a 36-year-old female, presents with a complaint of ankle pain and a history of diabetes and high blood pressure.
She reports that her ankle pain began two days ago after slipping and twisting her left ankle on the outside of her hip while walking on ice and snow.
She reports falling and being unable to walk due to significant pain and swelling.
She has tried ibuprofen, ice, and elevation for pain and swelling, but has only experienced minimal relief.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of diabetes and high blood pressure.
She is currently taking metformin and norvasc.
She reports that her blood sugars have been stable and she is managing her diabetes through diet and regular sugar checks.
She has an upcoming appointment for an eye exam and has had a hemoglobin A1c of 6.8 in the past.
Review of systems: The patient denies pain or discomfort in her calf, lower leg, or knee.
She reports that her ankle pain is localized to the outside of her ankle.
Current medications: I prescribed metformin for diabetes management and norvasc for high blood pressure management.
The patient reports needing a refill on her norvasc.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure is 120/70.
Physical exam: The patient's neck exam is normal, with no bruits.
Her lungs are clear, and her heart exam is normal, with a 2/6 systolic ejection murmur that is unchanged from previous exams.
Her belly exam is normal.
The ankle exam reveals tenderness over the lateral malleolus and the fifth metatarsal, with swelling and redness.
There is pain with valgus stressing of the ankle.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient likely has an ankle sprain, but I am concerned about the possibility of a fracture due to tenderness over the fifth metatarsal.
Problem list: Ankle sprain; Suspected fracture; Diabetes; High blood pressure
Plan:
I ordered an x-ray of the ankle to evaluate for a possible fracture.
I will put the patient in a splint and provide crutches until the x-ray results are available.
I prescribed naprosyn 500mg twice a day for pain control and instructed the patient to keep the ankle elevated and apply ice.
I will re-evaluate the patient after the x-ray results are available and adjust the treatment plan as needed.
Follow-up: I will contact the patient if the x-ray results show a fracture and refer her to orthopedics.
If the x-ray results are normal, I will have the patient follow up in a week to reassess the ankle and begin partial weight-bearing.
